title_en: The Quranic Statement and Its Implications
content_en:
The Quranic statement on the crucifixion — 'they did not kill him, nor did they crucify him; but [another] was made to resemble him to them' (4:157) — must be understood in its full theological context to appreciate both its significance and its implications. This verse is part of a longer passage that catalogues the crimes committed by those who rejected divine guidance among the Children of Israel: they broke their covenant, disbelieved in the signs of Allah, killed prophets unjustly, and claimed to have killed the Messiah Jesus, son of Mary. The Quranic refutation of this claim — 'they did not kill him' — serves both a theological and a psychological purpose: it vindicates Jesus from the dishonor of being killed by his enemies and affirms the divine protection that Allah extends to His messengers.

The Islamic theological tradition is unanimous that Jesus was not killed on the cross and that he was raised to Allah — to the divine presence, in a state of life — prior to any successful harm being done to him. The Quran states elsewhere: 'When Allah said: O Jesus, indeed I will take you and raise you to Myself and purify you from those who disbelieve' (3:55). The promise of divine protection was fulfilled: Jesus was 'taken' by Allah and raised to Him, and the Jewish claim to have killed the Messiah is described as false.

The verse's acknowledgment that 'those who differ over it are in doubt about it' is a remarkable statement about the state of knowledge among the contemporaries of these events. Even in the first generation after Jesus, the Quran suggests, there was genuine uncertainty and disagreement about what had happened. Some believed Jesus had died; others believed he had not. The Gnostic Christians of the early centuries — some of the earliest Christian communities — held docetic views that denied Jesus's physical death. The diversity of early Christian opinion on this matter is attested by historians of early Christianity and supports the Quranic suggestion that the matter was genuinely uncertain from the beginning.

The implications of the Quranic position are profound for the theology of both Islam and Christianity. For Islam, the question of what happened to Jesus is less central than it is for Christianity, because Islam does not locate salvation in the death and resurrection of a divine figure. Human beings are forgiven by Allah's mercy in response to sincere repentance and righteous action — they do not require a blood sacrifice or a vicarious atonement to secure divine forgiveness. The greatness of Jesus in Islam lies in his prophetic mission, his miraculous birth, his extraordinary character, and his role as the messenger who announced the coming of the final prophet — not in his death.

For the relationship between Islam and Christianity, the crucifixion question represents the deepest theological divide. Yet Deedat argues that this divide is not as absolute as it might appear. He notes that the Jesus of the Gospels himself — the Jesus who prays in the Garden of Gethsemane that 'if it be possible, let this cup pass from me' (Matthew 26:39) — does not welcome crucifixion as the fulfillment of his divine mission but expresses a deeply human desire to be delivered from it. This prayer of Jesus aligns, in spirit if not in theological interpretation, with the Islamic understanding of a prophet who sought and received divine rescue.

Deedat concludes that the question 'Was Jesus crucified?' is not merely an academic historical inquiry but a question with eternal significance. The answer one gives to it determines the entire architecture of one's theology: whether salvation requires a divine sacrifice or divine mercy; whether Jesus's mission was completed in his death or continues in his ongoing role as a prophet and in the final message of the Quran that his coming announced. The Islamic answer — that he was not crucified — reflects the Quranic vision of a God who protects His messengers, who does not abandon the righteous to the hands of their enemies, and who brings His divine plan to completion through the final prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him).
content_id:
Pernyataan Al-Quran tentang penyaliban — 'mereka tidak membunuhnya dan tidak menyalibnya, tetapi (yang mereka bunuh ialah) orang yang diserupakan dengan Isa bagi mereka' (4:157) — harus dipahami dalam konteks teologis penuhnya untuk menghargai baik signifikansi maupun implikasinya. Ayat ini adalah bagian dari bagian yang lebih panjang yang mendaftar kejahatan-kejahatan yang dilakukan oleh mereka yang menolak petunjuk ilahi di antara Bani Israel: mereka melanggar perjanjian mereka, tidak beriman kepada tanda-tanda Allah, membunuh para nabi secara tidak adil, dan mengklaim telah membunuh Al-Masih Isa putra Maryam. Penolakan Al-Quran atas klaim ini — 'mereka tidak membunuhnya' — melayani tujuan teologis maupun psikologis: ini membenarkan Isa dari kehinaan dibunuh oleh musuh-musuhnya dan menegaskan perlindungan ilahi yang Allah berikan kepada para rasul-Nya.

Tradisi teologi Islam bulat bahwa Isa tidak dibunuh di kayu salib dan bahwa ia diangkat kepada Allah — kepada kehadiran ilahi, dalam keadaan hidup — sebelum bahaya apa pun berhasil menimpanya. Al-Quran menyatakan di tempat lain: 'Ketika Allah berfirman: Hai Isa, sesungguhnya Aku akan menyampaikan kamu kepada akhir ajalmu dan mengangkat kamu kepada-Ku serta membersihkan kamu dari orang-orang yang kafir' (3:55). Janji perlindungan ilahi terpenuhi: Isa 'diambil' oleh Allah dan diangkat kepada-Nya, dan klaim Yahudi telah membunuh Al-Masih digambarkan sebagai palsu.

Pengakuan ayat bahwa 'orang-orang yang berselisih paham tentang itu benar-benar dalam keragu-raguan tentangnya' adalah pernyataan yang luar biasa tentang keadaan pengetahuan di antara orang-orang sezaman dengan peristiwa-peristiwa ini. Bahkan pada generasi pertama setelah Isa, Al-Quran menyiratkan, terdapat ketidakpastian dan ketidaksepakatan yang nyata tentang apa yang telah terjadi. Beberapa percaya Isa telah meninggal; yang lain percaya ia tidak. Orang-orang Kristen Gnostik pada abad-abad awal — beberapa komunitas Kristen paling awal — memegang pandangan-pandangan doketik yang menyangkal kematian fisik Isa. Keberagaman pendapat Kristen awal tentang masalah ini dibuktikan oleh sejarawan Kekristenan awal dan mendukung saran Al-Quran bahwa masalah tersebut memang tidak pasti sejak awal.

Implikasi posisi Al-Quran sangat mendalam bagi teologi Islam maupun Kristen. Bagi Islam, pertanyaan tentang apa yang terjadi pada Isa kurang sentral dibandingkan dengan Kristen, karena Islam tidak menempatkan keselamatan pada kematian dan kebangkitan tokoh ilahi. Manusia diampuni oleh rahmat Allah sebagai respons terhadap taubat yang tulus dan amal saleh — mereka tidak memerlukan pengorbanan darah atau penebusan wakil untuk mendapatkan pengampunan ilahi. Keagungan Isa dalam Islam terletak pada misi kenabiannya, kelahiran mukjizatnya, karakternya yang luar biasa, dan perannya sebagai utusan yang mengumumkan kedatangan nabi terakhir — bukan pada kematiannya.

Untuk hubungan antara Islam dan Kristen, pertanyaan penyaliban mewakili jurang teologis yang paling dalam. Namun Deedat berargumen bahwa jurang ini tidak sesebsolut yang mungkin tampak. Ia mencatat bahwa Isa dari Injil sendiri — Isa yang berdoa di Taman Getsemani bahwa 'jika sekiranya mungkin, biarlah cawan ini lalu daripada-Ku' (Matius 26:39) — tidak menyambut penyaliban sebagai pemenuhan misi ilahinya melainkan mengungkapkan keinginan yang sangat manusiawi untuk dilepaskan dari itu. Doa Isa ini selaras, dalam ruh jika bukan dalam interpretasi teologis, dengan pemahaman Islam tentang seorang nabi yang mencari dan menerima penyelamatan ilahi.

Deedat menyimpulkan bahwa pertanyaan 'Apakah Isa disalib?' bukan sekadar penyelidikan historis akademis melainkan pertanyaan dengan signifikansi abadi. Jawaban yang seseorang berikan atasnya menentukan seluruh arsitektur teologinya: apakah keselamatan memerlukan pengorbanan ilahi atau rahmat ilahi; apakah misi Isa diselesaikan dalam kematiannya atau berlanjut dalam perannya yang berkelanjutan sebagai nabi dan dalam pesan terakhir Al-Quran yang kedatangannya diumumkan. Jawaban Islam — bahwa ia tidak disalib — mencerminkan visi Al-Quran tentang Tuhan yang melindungi para rasul-Nya, yang tidak meninggalkan orang-orang saleh kepada tangan musuh-musuh mereka, dan yang membawa rencana ilahi-Nya kepada penyelesaian melalui nabi terakhir Muhammad ﷺ.
title_ar: هل صُلب عيسى — الخاتمة والحق المبين
content_ar: The القرآني statement on the crucifixion — 'they did not kill him, nor did they crucify him; but [another] was made to resemble him to them' (4:157) — must be understood in its full theological context to appreciate both its significance and its implications. This الآية is part of a longer passage that catalogues the crimes committed by those who rejected divine الهداية among the الأولاد of Israel: they broke their covenant, disbelieved in the signs of الله, killed الأنبياء unjustly, and claimed to have killed the Messiah عيسى ﷺ, son of Mary. The القرآني refutation of this claim — 'they did not kill him' — serves both a theological and a psychological purpose: it vindicates عيسى ﷺ from the dishonor of being killed by his enemies and affirms the divine protection that الله extends to His الرسل. The Islamic theological التراث is unanimous that عيسى ﷺ was not killed on the cross and that he was raised to الله — to the divine presence, in a state of الحياة — prior to any successful harm being done to him. The القرآن الكريم states elsewhere: 'عندما الله said: O عيسى ﷺ, indeed I will take you and raise you to Myself and purify you from those who disbelieve' (3:55). The promise of divine protection was fulfilled: عيسى ﷺ was 'taken' by الله and raised to Him, and the Jewish claim to have killed the Messiah is described as false. The الآية's acknowledgment that 'those who differ over it are in doubt about it' is a remarkable statement about the state of العلم among the contemporaries of these events. Even in the أولاً generation بعد عيسى ﷺ, the القرآن الكريم suggests, there was genuine uncertainty and disagreement about what had happened. Some believed عيسى ﷺ had died; others believed he had not. The Gnostic Christians of the early centuries — some of the earliest Christian communities — held docetic views that denied عيسى ﷺ's physical الموت. The diversity of early Christian opinion on this matter is attested by historians of early Christianity and supports the القرآني suggestion that the matter was genuinely uncertain from the beginning. The implications of the القرآني position are profound for the theology of both الإسلام and Christianity. For الإسلام, the question of what happened to عيسى ﷺ is less المحوري than it is for Christianity, لأن الإسلام does not locate النجاة in the الموت and البعث of a divine figure. Human beings are forgiven by الله's الرحمة in response to sincere التوبة and الصالح action — they do not require a blood sacrifice or a vicarious atonement to secure divine المغفرة. The greatness of عيسى ﷺ in الإسلام lies in his prophetic mission, his miraculous birth, his extraordinary الأخلاق, and his role as الرسول ﷺ who announced the coming of the final النبي — not in his الموت. For the relationship between الإسلام and Christianity, the crucifixion question represents the deepest theological divide. Yet Deedat argues that this divide is not as absolute as it might appear.
